hero.tagline: Oklahoma's small claims court offers a streamlined process for disputes up to $10,000, with hearings typically scheduled within 30 to 60 days.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in district court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $10,000. The statute of limitations varies: 5 years for written contracts and 3 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in six months, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a colleague $1,500 with the understanding they'd repay in three months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Gather any written agreement, payment records, and communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $5,000 over a year but stopped making payments after two months.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $3,000 to a client who hasn't paid the invoice issued two months ago.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, contract (if any), and communication regarding the services.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $2,500 on their store credit account and has stopped making payments.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from last payment or charge. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,000 in unpaid rent over the past four months and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $1,200 worth of goods with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from date of check. Provide the bounced check, bank notice, and any related communication.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $6,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now you're being pursued for repayment.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement, proof of payments made, and any communication with the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $10,000, including issues with products, services, and contracts.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 refrigerator that stopped working after two months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of defect. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but left your home in poor condition, and they refuse to refund or redo the job.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix your car's issue, and they refuse to correct the problem.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment records, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $7,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they left the job unfinished and subpar.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from completion date. Provide the contract, payment proof, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a DJ for your wedding for $1,200, but they never showed up and refuse to refund your payment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from event date. Bring the contract, payment records, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a gym membership, but they continued to charge your account $50 monthly for six months.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation, bank statements, and communication with the gym.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from purchase date. Bring the bill of sale, any warranty, and repair estimates.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A store advertised a laptop for $800 with specific features, but the model you received lacked those features.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from purchase date. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and communication with the seller.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $10,000, including vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and caused $6,000 in damage to your car.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $4,000 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the shop.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court handles monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without providing an itemized list of deductions.
claims.notes: Landlords must return deposits within 45 days or provide an itemized statement. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without specifying the reasons.
claims.notes: Landlords must provide an itemized statement for any deductions. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: Landlords must maintain habitable premises. Bring photos of the conditions, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture valued at $3,000 without legal justification.
claims.notes: Landlords must follow proper procedures for handling tenant property. Bring the lease agreement, inventory of items, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the changed locks, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but after moving out early, the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: Landlords must return unearned rent. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed three months of rent totaling $3,600 and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You fixed a broken heater costing $500 and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord is disputing this.
claims.notes: Tenants may repair and deduct under certain conditions. Bring repair receipts, photos, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court addresses employment-related disputes up to $10,000, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued payment.
claims.notes: Employers must pay wages due. Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: Final wages are due by the next regular payday. Bring resignation notice, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: Commissions are considered wages. Bring sales records, commission agreements, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime last month, but your employer didn't pay the additional wages.
claims.notes: Overtime must be paid as required by law. Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court handles personal disputes up to $10,000, including minor injuries and property issues.

claims.name: Minor personal injury claim
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in a $2,000 medical bill.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Bring medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,200 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from demand for return. Provide proof of ownership, communication with the borrower, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft date. Bring police reports, proof of ownership, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Oklahoma's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000, including mechanic issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,500 for repairs that didn't fix your car's issue, and they refuse to correct it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment records, and any communication with the mechanic.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $500 to release it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from towing date. Provide photos of the parking spot, towing receipt, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled under a different procedure and are not suitable for small claims court.
Disputes involving ownership or title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for libel or slander are explicitly excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
Professional malpractice claims require complex evidence and are not suitable for small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are handled by specialized courts.
Claims involving federal law or against the federal government are outside the jurisdiction of state small claims courts.
Class action lawsuits are complex and not suitable for the simplified procedures of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Such claims fall under federal jurisdiction and cannot be heard in state small claims court.
Claims for workplace injuries are handled by the Workers' Compensation Commission.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are typically not eligible for small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable if specified by statute or contract; otherwise, each party typically bears their own fees.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local court clerk for specific information.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk for specifics.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: For unpaid wage claims, a written demand is recommended to establish a clear record of the request for payment.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 45 days; failure to do so may result in penalties.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Certain consumer protection claims may require prior notice to the seller; consult the Oklahoma Consumer Protection Act for specifics.

forms.name: Small Claims Affidavit
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the date of the hearing.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's claims.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Enables the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Motion to Vacate Judgment
forms.description: Requests the court to set aside a default judgment.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a judgment.

forms.name: Pauper's Affidavit
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Court clerk sends the papers via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Oklahoma's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication as a last resort.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, you may request alternative methods of service from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the hearing to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim before a judgment is granted.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the denial within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds $10,000, the case may be transferred to the regular civil docket.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial where both parties present evidence and testimony.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; the court does not provide them.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few days

appeals.type_notes: The appeal results in a new trial in the district court.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The bond amount is set by the court and typically covers the judgment amount and costs.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but the appellant must show good cause for failing to appear.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues from the date of judgment until paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File an application for wage garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank will freeze and turn over funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a certified copy of the judgment with the county clerk where the debtor owns property, creating a lien that must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it at public auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; if granted, the judgment is extended for another 5 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are filed.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a statement with the court.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may send a designated employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts in court.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Oklahoma's rules for service of process carefully, using authorized methods.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of adequate evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't respond, you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Address any counterclaims promptly and prepare a defense.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Learn about collection methods like garnishment and liens, and act promptly.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Oklahoma small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases in Oklahoma are typically scheduled for hearing within 30 to 60 days after filing, providing a relatively quick resolution compared to other court processes.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Oklahoma small claims court is $10,000, excluding attorney fees and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may grant a default judgment in your favor. However, you must still present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment by filing a notice of appeal within 30 days. The appeal results in a new trial (trial de novo) in the district court.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Oklahoma is valid for 5 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Oklahoma allows businesses to represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
Eviction cases are handled under a different procedure and are not suitable for small claims court.
Defamation claims, including libel and slander, are explicitly excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
The statute of limitations for written contracts is 5 years, while for oral contracts it is 3 years.
Small claims judgments can be appealed within 30 days, resulting in a new trial in the district court.

interesting_findings: Oklahoma's small claims court allows for a relatively high monetary limit of $10,000 compared to some other states.
The process is designed to be accessible for individuals without legal representation, promoting self-representation.
Certain claims, such as those involving title to real estate or family law matters, are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.

sources.label: Okla. Stat. Title 12 § 1751 et seq. (Small Claims Procedure Act)
Oklahoma Rules of Civil Procedure (via OSCN)
Okla. Stat. Title 15 § 751 et seq. (Consumer Protection Act)
